items.title: Northwind Dashboard
items.description: Analytics UI for a logistics team — reduced time-to-insight with a restrained component library and shared design tokens.

items.title: Studio Meridian
items.description: Brand and marketing site for a small architecture practice. Static-first build, editorial typography, subtle scroll cues.

items.title: Pulse Reader
items.description: RSS-style reading app prototype focused on offline use, legibility modes, and keyboard-first navigation.

items.title: apple
items.description: apple is a fruit, eat it and keep doctors away

items.title: Sparky
items.description: Sparky is a powerful troop in Clash Royale that throws a powerful spark of electricity to the enemy that vaporizes the enemy within a fraction of a second.

source: ### in order to work with date column properly, we need to change the datatype to datetime which is integer datatype initially. Other columns seems to be alright

# First convert to numeric, then to datetime
df_2['DATE'] = pd.to_numeric(df_2['DATE'])
df_2['DATE'] = pd.to_datetime(df_2['DATE'], unit='D', origin='1899-12-30')
df_2.info()

source: ### Since we are working with chips category, we need to remove unnecessary or irrelevant products such as salsa in this case

# Filter out rows containing "salsa" (case insensitive)
df_2_filtered = df_2[~df_2["PROD_NAME"].str.lower().str.contains("salsa")]

# If you want to filter out multiple items, you can use:
# items_to_remove = ["salsa", "other_item", "another_item"]
# df_2_filtered = df_2[~df_2["PROD_NAME"].str.lower().isin([item.lower() for item in items_to_remove])]

# Check the results after filtering
print("\nAfter filtering:")
print(df_2_filtered["PROD_NAME"].unique())
print(f"Total rows: {len(df_2_filtered)}")

# Assign the filtered DataFrame back to df_2 if you want to replace the original
df_2 = df_2_filtered.copy()

### We checked for outliers using .describe function and we found that the highest product quantity is 200 which is unusual and therefore we will check for how many other purchase are of similar kind and if there is not many, we will remove those outliers

source: ### In transaction dataset, we have the product weight of the product is written aling with the full product description. We will now create a new column and extract the weight of the product to the new column.
### Similarly we will extract the Brand name to a seperate column, which is usually the first word of the description, but if it is not, we will change it later manually with the correct one

df_2['WEIGHT'] = df_2['PROD_NAME'].str.extract(r'(\d+)[gG]')

df_2['WEIGHT'] = df_2['WEIGHT'].astype('object')

df_2['PROD_BRAND'] = df_2['PROD_NAME'].str.split().str[0]
df_2['PROD_BRAND'] = df_2['PROD_BRAND'].astype('object')

source: ### Here the brand names seems to be repeating because of spelling errors or the use of abbrevriations therefore, we will replace the incorrect or the repeated one with single unique product brand name

df_2['PROD_BRAND'] = df_2['PROD_BRAND'].replace({
    'Natural': 'NCC',
    'WW':'Woolworths',
    'Smith': 'Smiths',
    'GrnWves' : 'Grain Waves',
    'Grain' : 'Grain Waves',
    'Infzns': 'Infuzions',
    'Red': 'RRD',
    'Snbts': 'Sunbites',
    'Burger': 'Burger Rings',
    'Dorito': 'Doritos'
})
df_2['PROD_BRAND'].unique()

source:
# Create complete date range
full_date_range = pd.date_range(start=df_2['DATE'].min(), end=df_2['DATE'].max(), freq='D')

# Find dates in dataset
existing_dates = df_2['DATE'].unique()

# Find missing dates
missing_dates = full_date_range.difference(existing_dates)
print(f"Missing date(s): {missing_dates}")

### until now we cleaned the dataset and made it ready perform other actions. now on we will work with matplotlib library to analyze and visualize the data. We will start with extracting the general information

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

monthly_sales = df_2.groupby(df_2['DATE'].dt.to_period('M'))['TOT_SALES'].sum()

plt.figure(figsize=(12,6))
monthly_sales.plot(kind='bar')
plt.title('Total Sales by Month')
plt.xlabel('Month')
plt.ylabel('Total Sales')
plt.show()
## Total sales on the month of December

december_data = df_2[df_2['DATE'].dt.month==12]

december_daily_sales = december_data.groupby('DATE')['TOT_SALES'].sum()
plt.figure(figsize=(12,6))
december_daily_sales.plot(kind='bar')
plt.title('Total Sales by day on december')
plt.xlabel('Days')
plt.ylabel('Total Sales')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

transaction_by_packsize = df_2.groupby('WEIGHT')['TOT_SALES'].sum()

plt.figure(figsize=(12,6))
transaction_by_packsize.plot(kind='bar')
plt.title('Transaction By Packsize')
plt.xlabel('Packsize')
plt.ylabel('Total Sales')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Number of customers in different sectors

customers_by_sector = df.groupby('PREMIUM_CUSTOMER')['PREMIUM_CUSTOMER'].count()

plt.figure(figsize=(12,6))
customers_by_sector.plot(kind='bar')
plt.title('customers by sector')
plt.xlabel('Customer sector')
plt.ylabel('Total Number')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)
for i, v in enumerate(customers_by_sector):
    plt.text(i,v,f"{v:,.0f}", ha='center',va='bottom', fontsize=10)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

transaction_by_Brand = df_2.groupby('PROD_BRAND')['TOT_SALES'].sum()
plt.figure(figsize=(12,6))
transaction_by_Brand.plot(kind='bar')
plt.title('Transaction By Brand')
plt.xlabel('Brand')
plt.ylabel('Total Sales')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

## until now we worked on the transaction dataset in order to extract the basic information such as highest selling brand, highest selling packsize etc. but from now we will merge the customer dataset and transaction dataset and dig down for more important trends or behaviours

source: ### Price per unit is an important aspect while analyzing a transaction dataset as it will help to derive some of the important information form the dataset. But we don't have price per unit for our data so, we well use the existing informations to derive the price per unit. In this case we will use product quantity and total sales.

## We will now work on a specific segment. i.e Mainstream young singles/couples and midage singles/couples to analyze their customer behaviour. we will ren a t-test to find out if their pruchasing behaviour is significantly comparable with other segment which may be because of some reasons or is the difference is just a coincidence or is insignificant.

# Independent t-test
t_statistic, p_value = stats.ttest_ind(mainstream, budget_premium)

print(f"\nT-Test Results:")
print(f"T-statistic: {t_statistic:.4f}")
print(f"P-value: {p_value:.6f}")

# Interpret results
if p_value < 0.05:
    print(f"\n✓ SIGNIFICANT: Mainstream young/midage singles/couples pay significantly")
    print(f"  {'MORE' if mainstream.mean() > budget_premium.mean() else 'LESS'} than Budget/Premium customers (p < 0.05)")
else:
    print(f"\n✗ NOT SIGNIFICANT: No significant difference in price per unit (p > 0.05)")

source: ### We will now analyze the mainstream young singles/couples affinity index which tells that how the segment is performing differntly comparing to the other segments.
### Affinity > 1 = Target segment prefers this brand MORE than others
### Affinity < 1 = Target segment prefers this brand LESS than others
## Brand preference for mainstream young singles/couples segment

# Step 1: Filter for target segment
target_segment = df[(df['LIFESTAGE'] == 'YOUNG SINGLES/COUPLES') & 
                    (df['PREMIUM_CUSTOMER'] == 'Mainstream')]
# Step 2: Filter for everyone else (comparison group)
other_segments = df[~((df['LIFESTAGE'] == 'YOUNG SINGLES/COUPLES') & 
                      (df['PREMIUM_CUSTOMER'] == 'Mainstream'))]
# Step 3: Calculate quantity sold by brand for each group
target_brand_qty = target_segment.groupby('PROD_BRAND')['PROD_QTY'].sum()
other_brand_qty = other_segments.groupby('PROD_BRAND')['PROD_QTY'].sum()

# Step 4: Calculate proportions (percentages)
target_brand_prop = target_brand_qty / target_brand_qty.sum()
other_brand_prop = other_brand_qty / other_brand_qty.sum()

# Step 5: Calculate affinity index
affinity = target_brand_prop / other_brand_prop
# Step 6: Sort and visualize
affinity_sorted = affinity.sort_values(ascending=False)
print(affinity_sorted)

# Plot brands with highest affinity (top preferences)
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
affinity_sorted.head(10).plot(kind='bar', color='steelblue')
plt.title('Brand Affinity for Mainstream Young Singles/Couples')
plt.xlabel('Brand')
plt.ylabel('Affinity Index (>1 = preferred more)')
plt.axhline(y=1, color='red', linestyle='--', label='Baseline')
plt.legend()
plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha='right')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
### pack size preferences for the target segment compared to overall population

#filtering the target segment with other segments
target_segment = df[(df['LIFESTAGE'] == 'YOUNG SINGLES/COUPLES')& (df['PREMIUM_CUSTOMER'] == 'Mainstream')]
other_segments = df[~((df['LIFESTAGE'] == 'YOUNG SINGLES/COUPLES')& (df['PREMIUM_CUSTOMER'] == 'Mainstream'))]

#calculating the sum of each segments
target_pack_size_qty = target_segment.groupby('WEIGHT')['PROD_QTY'].sum()
other_pack_size_qty = other_segments.groupby('WEIGHT')['PROD_QTY'].sum()

#finding the propertion of target segment and the remaining segment
target_seg_prop = target_pack_size_qty / target_pack_size_qty.sum()
other_seg_prop = other_pack_size_qty / other_pack_size_qty.sum()

#finding the affinity index
affinity = target_seg_prop / other_seg_prop

#sorting the affinity 
affinity_sorted = affinity.sort_values(ascending = False)
print(affinity_sorted)

# Executive Summary

## Key Findings
1. **Best Performing Month:** December
2. **Best Performing Day:** 24th December(Christmas eve)
3. **Most Sold Pack Size:** 175 grams
4. **Most Sold Brand:** Kettle
5. **Sales Leaders:** Budget Older Families and Mainstream Young Singles/Couples drive 45% of total sales
6. **Price Sensitivity:** Mainstream segment pays 8% more per unit than Budget (statistically significant, p<0.001)
7. **Brand Preferences:** Young Singles/Couples show 23% higher affinity for Kettle brand

## Recommendations
1. Increase promotional activity targeting Budget Older Families
2. Offer larger pack sizes for Older Families segment
